does: This paper introduces CoVUBench, a comprehensive benchmark for evaluating machine unlearning in large vision-language models, specifically targeting the removal of copyrighted visual content like characters and logos. The authors develop a multimodal evaluation framework that uses procedurally generated synthetic data with systematic visual variations to assess how effectively copyright content can be erased from LVLMs post-training. Their protocol simultaneously measures two key dimensions: forgetting efficacy, which quantifies how completely copyrighted concepts are removed from the model's responses and internal representations, and utility preservation, which ensures that unlearning does not degrade the model's general capabilities and performance on non-target content. The benchmark spans compositional variations and domain-specific manifestations to provide realistic and generalizable evaluation of unlearning robustness.
connects: This work builds on the emerging field of machine unlearning, which extends prior work on data poisoning, adversarial robustness, and interpretability in neural networks. It responds directly to the challenge of copyright and intellectual property protection in foundation models, a problem heightened by the scale and web-derived training data of modern LVLMs. The paper connects to earlier unlearning literature that focused on tabular or text-only settings by extending these methods to the complex multimodal domain where concepts must be erased across both visual and textual representations. It also engages with evaluation challenges documented in model memorization studies and benchmarking work for vision-language models, synthesizing insights from both copyright compliance and general model evaluation literature.
enables: This benchmark enables future research into more effective unlearning algorithms tailored for multimodal content by providing a rigorous, standardized evaluation framework. It supports the development of copyright-aware training and post-training techniques that balance content removal with model utility, addressing a critical need for trustworthy deployment of LVLMs in regulated environments. The work opens pathways for evaluating unlearning at scale across diverse copyrighted concepts and their visual manifestations. It also enables exploration of fundamental questions about the mechanisms of cross-modal concept representation in LVLMs and whether unlearning in one modality automatically transfers to others. Beyond copyright, the framework could support unlearning of other sensitive content categories, contributing to safer and more responsible deployment of foundation models.
